results.title: Arkor: Fine-tune and Deploy Open-weight Models in ...
results.content:
Subscribe

Launching today

Arkor

# Arkor

Launching today

## Fine-tune and Deploy Open-weight Models in TypeScript

18 followers

## Fine-tune and Deploy Open-weight Models in TypeScript

18 followers

Visit website

Engineering & Development

Start a real LLM training in 10 minutes. Tell Claude Code or Codex what the model is for; they prepare the datasets and create the training project. Click "Run Training" in the local studio. Arkor runs the training, and deploys the trained model as an OpenAI-compatible API. Think Next.js and Vercel for fine-tuning: code you can review, infrastructure you do not have to manage, and a model your app can call. No GPU setup. No ML expertise required. No Python training code to write.

 Overview
 Reviews
 Alternatives
 Built with
 Team
 More

Free [...] With Arkor, the workflow starts inside your existing repository:

1. Tell Claude Code or Codex what behavior you want from the model.
2. Your coding agent can find or prepare a dataset, write conversion scripts, create the TypeScript trainer, and add evaluation.
3. Review the generated code and changes.
4. The agent runs pnpm dev, and Arkor Studio opens locally at localhost:4000.
5. Click Run Training, monitor the loss and checkpoints, test the trained adapter, and deploy the result.

For example, you can give your coding agent a prompt like:

> Use Arkor to fine-tune a model that rewrites rough drafts as tweets in my style:   
> Find or prepare a suitable dataset, create the TypeScript training workflow, and tell me when it is ready to review in Arkor Studio. [...] Arkor is not intended to be a magical prompt-to-model black box. It is a developer-controlled workflow where coding agents can handle much of the setup, while the training code, data transformations, evaluation, and final decisions remain visible and reviewable.

We’re especially interested in feedback on:

 Whether the coding-agent workflow feels intuitive
 Which parts of fine-tuning still feel unclear or intimidating
 What you would need before using Arkor for a production model
 Which models, datasets, and deployment workflows we should support next

Thanks for checking out Arkor. We’ll be here throughout the launch and would love to hear what you try building. 🙏

Brandon TK Beesman

results.title: Build a Production-Ready TypeScript Codegen SLM in ...
results.content:
The same pattern can be applied to other stacks (e.g., Python, Go, or Java backends) with new datasets and filters.

## Closing Thoughts

Building a focused TypeScript SLM is achievable with modern open-source tooling and modest hardware. This workflow provides a repeatable method to collect TypeScript-specific datasets, fine-tune small models, and deploy them as local GGUFs for fast inference.

Running the full pipeline with:

python slm.py pipeline
This is enough to produce a usable model in a single afternoon, and the setup is straightforward to adjust as your requirements evolve.

## References

## Model Releases & Source code

   TypeScript SLM 7B Reasoning (Hugging Face)

   TypeScript SLM 1.5B (Hugging Face)

   SLM TypeScript Model — Full Source Code (GitHub) [...] ## Colab Shortcuts

For cloud-based runs:

!python colab/colab_train_and_upload.py # 1.5B full pipeline

!python colab/colab_train_7b.py --variant standard
These notebooks encapsulate the same logic as the CLI, but are tuned for Colab environments.

## Deployment to Hugging Face and Ollama

Deploy a model in a single step:

python slm.py deploy typescript-slm-1.5b
This command:

   Merges LoRA weights into the base model
   Converts the model to GGUF
   Generates Modelfiles
   Pushes artifacts to Hugging Face

For local-only usage:

python slm.py merge typescript-slm-1.5b

python slm.py convert typescript-slm-1.5b --quant q4_k_m

python slm.py modelfile typescript-slm-1.5b
## Evaluation Snapshot

The models were evaluated on 100 TypeScript-focused tasks: [...] Framework-aware generators for React, Next.js, Angular, and Node.js
   LoRA-based fine-tuning that fits T4, A100, and M-series Macs
   A unified CLI (`slm.py`) that handles collect → preprocess → train → evaluate → deploy
   Filtered dataset tiers (small, medium, ultra)
   Modelfiles with system prompts for easy Ollama deployment
   GGUF-first pipeline for `llama.cpp` and local inference

This is designed for reproducibility and practical usage, not benchmark chasing.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image 4

TypeScript — SLM Trainer CLI interface

## Project Structure

slm.py # Typer CLI with all commands

scripts/ # Training, filtering, evaluation utilities

colab/ # One-command training notebooks

data/processed/ # JSONL splits (excluded from version control)

results.title: Best open source models for SWE-Bench coding agents
results.content:
Open-weight, permissively licensed models offer transparency, customization potential, and freedom from vendor lock-in. With licenses like Apache 2.0 and MIT, teams can fine-tune models on proprietary codebases, deploy them in air-gapped environments, and audit model behavior. Models like Qwen3-Coder-Next achieving 70.6% on SWE-bench Verified demonstrate that open-weight alternatives now compete with proprietary systems.

### How do models like Qwen3-Coder-Next and DeepSeek-V3.2 differ in their core functionalities? [...] ### Developer Experience

Modal's code-first SDK enables code-defined infrastructure with no YAML, with support for Python, TypeScript, and Go. Teams define compute requirements directly in code, enabling rapid iteration as coding agent architectures evolve.

For teams deploying the open-weight models in this guide, whether Qwen3-Coder-Next for parameter-efficient inference or DeepSeek-V3.2 for reasoning-heavy tasks, Modal provides the infrastructure foundation that makes production deployment practical.

Explore the Modal documentation to get started with coding agent deployment.

Explore the Modal documentation to get started with coding agent deployment.

View Modal Docs

## Frequently asked questions

### What is a SWE-Bench-style coding agent? [...] ### Security and Compliance

Modal has completed SOC 2 Type II and supports HIPAA-compliant workloads on Enterprise plans via a BAA. The platform uses TLS 1.3 for public APIs and encryption for data in transit and at rest.

### Why Teams Choose Modal for Coding Agents

Modal powers cloud infrastructure for over 10,000 teams, including AI companies building coding agents at scale. The platform's code-first SDK eliminates YAML configuration, with code-defined infrastructure available in Python, TypeScript, and Go, enabling teams to define compute requirements directly in code.

Best For: Teams deploying open-weight coding models to production, running model evaluations at scale, or building coding agents that need secure execution environments with on-demand GPU access.
